coredata.dc:title: Double-reverse-signal ratiometric fluorescence probe for residual determination of chlorpyrifos pesticide in surface water based on scalable upcycling of rice straw to luminescent CQDs via energy-efficient alkali treatment

coredata.dc:description: A smart, green, practical, and simple method has been developed for the preparation of blue emissive carbon dots (RS-CDs). The developed synthesis protocol is accomplished at room temperature via an instrument-free approach using the alkali treatment of rice straw. The furnished RS-CDs were thoroughly characterized via transmission electron microscopy, energy-dispersive X-ray spectrometry, x-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, UV-Vis, and fluorescence spectroscopy. The RS-CDs exhibited excellent performance as a double-response-reverse type ratiometric fluorescence probe for the widely used insecticide chlorpyrifos. By systematically investigating and optimizing the experimental conditions, a new sensitive and accurate RS-CDs-based ratiometric fluorescence probe was constructed, taking advantage of the simultaneous decrease of emission intensity at 396 nm and increase of a new emission peak at 340 nm upon adding chlorpyrifos. The ratio of fluorescence intensity at 340 and 396 nm (F340/396) showed excellent correlation to chlorpyrifos concentration over the range of 7.1 – 142.4 µM with a detection limit of 2.3 µM. Excellent recovery percentages have been obtained for chlorpyrifos in tap, river, and irrigation water, 102.24 ± 1.52, 104.33 ± 1.79, and 100.43 ± 2.51 %, respectively. The presented approach is the first to convert agricultural waste into nanomaterial by room temperature treatment. Boosting the value of rice straw via upcycling to luminescent CDs is one of the things that helps sustain the practice in limited-income labs. The environmental impact, greenness, practicality, economical, and analytical attributes of the developed method have been positively confirmed by different recent metrics.

head.ce:title: Double-reverse-signal ratiometric fluorescence probe for residual determination of chlorpyrifos pesticide in surface water based on scalable upcycling of rice straw to luminescent CQDs via energy-efficient alkali treatment

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A smart, green, practical, and simple method has been developed for the preparation of blue emissive carbon dots (RS-CDs). The developed synthesis protocol is accomplished at room temperature via an instrument-free approach using the alkali treatment of rice straw. The furnished RS-CDs were thoroughly characterized via transmission electron microscopy, energy-dispersive X-ray spectrometry, x-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, UV-Vis, and fluorescence spectroscopy. The RS-CDs exhibited excellent performance as a double-response-reverse type ratiometric fluorescence probe for the widely used insecticide chlorpyrifos. By systematically investigating and optimizing the experimental conditions, a new sensitive and accurate RS-CDs-based ratiometric fluorescence probe was constructed, taking advantage of the simultaneous decrease of emission intensity at 396 nm and increase of a new emission peak at 340 nm upon adding chlorpyrifos. The ratio of fluorescence intensity at 340 and 396 nm (F340/396) showed excellent correlation to chlorpyrifos concentration over the range of 7.1 – 142.4 µM with a detection limit of 2.3 µM. Excellent recovery percentages have been obtained for chlorpyrifos in tap, river, and irrigation water, 102.24 ± 1.52, 104.33 ± 1.79, and 100.43 ± 2.51 %, respectively. The presented approach is the first to convert agricultural waste into nanomaterial by room temperature treatment. Boosting the value of rice straw via upcycling to luminescent CDs is one of the things that helps sustain the practice in limited-income labs. The environmental impact, greenness, practicality, economical, and analytical attributes of the developed method have been positively confirmed by different recent metrics.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: Pesticides are a group of compounds, including insecticides, fungicides, herbicides, and rodenticides, that are used for the protection of crops, upgrading their productivity, controlling disease, and enhancing the quality of food. A pesticide must be deadly to the pursued pests but not to other species or humans [1]. However, this is not the case for all pesticides since some of them are highly poisonous to humans, plants, birds, and beneficial organisms, and a small quantity may lead to harmful impacts ranging from slight symptoms, such as skin irritation or allergy, to severe symptoms such as strong headache, dizziness, or nausea. Some pesticides, such as organophosphates, can initiate serious symptoms such as convulsions, coma, and even death. Besides, numerous pesticides can accumulate in living organisms, leading to chronic influences [2].

Surface water may be polluted with pesticides through overflow from treated plants and soil. This phenomenon is prevalent, and it is augmented by the high water-solubility of pesticides that increases the risk of leaching. Furthermore, irrigation increases the risk of pesticide migration from saturated soils to surface water and groundwater. Chlorpyrifos (CPS, O,O-diethyl O-(3,5,6-trichloropyridin-2-yl) phosphorothioate) is one of the organophosphate pesticides widely used in about 100 countries to control pests such as insects and worms. Exposure of humans to high levels of CPS leads to developmental, neurological, and autoimmune disorders. It causes major toxicities such as acetylcholine esterase inhibition, oxidative stress, and endocrine disruption [3]. This raised the demand for a rapid, precise, and sensitive sensor to detect CPS in water. This sensor will help protect humans and living organisms from the serious adverse effects of exposure to CPS. In this context, analytical methods reported for CPS detection in water include gas chromatography (GC) [4–6], high-performance liquid chromatography-UV [7], ultra-performance liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (UPLC-MS/MS) [8], electrochemical [9–11], functionalized paper-based device [12], fluorimetry [13–20], and colorimetry [21,22]. Yet, these techniques suffer from some limitations. For instance, the GC [4–6] and UPLC-MS/MS [8] methods require long analysis and complex expensive instruments. Besides, the electrochemical methods [9–11] are very time-consuming since they need lengthy time and high temperatures (1–7 days at 160–180 ºC) for electrode material synthesis. Moreover, the fluorimetry methods for CPS [13–20] are mostly based on nanomaterials that necessitate high temperatures (60–200 ºC) and long times (5–24 h) for synthesis of the probes, which hinders their applicability or depend on a chemical probe derived from harmful compounds. In addition, the colorimetry methods for CPS utilize metal nanoparticles, such as cobalt [21] and copper [22] nanoparticles, that are known for their harmful effects [23]. These facts explain the need for a faster, simpler, more feasible, and more sustainable analytical method for CPS. For this reason, spectrofluorimetry method that overcome these limitations appears to be a very suited technique owing to its high sensitivity, convenience, simplicity, and instrument availability [24–26].

In this context, many fluorescent nanomaterials emerged as an efficient and sensitive fluorescence sensor that is a superior alternative to derivatizing agents, dyes, and labeling fluorophores. Interestingly, carbon-based nanomaterials show excellent performance as sensitive, non-toxic, stable, biocompatible, and highly fluorescent nanosensors for various applications in drug [24–31], environmental [32–35], and biological [36,37] analysis. Fluorescence of carbon-based nanomaterials is likely originating from quantum confinement or emissive domains [38]. Different synthesis approaches are currently applied to the synthesis of carbon-based nanomaterials such as carbon dots (CDs), which can be grouped into two broad classes: top-bottom and bottom-up. The first approach depends on the decomposition of the carbonaceous substrate into nanosized particles, while the second approach depends on building nanoparticles from small molecules. The substrates utilized as carbon sources for the synthesis of CDs vary greatly, ranging from chemical compounds like citric acid, ascorbic acid, glucose, and ethylenediamine to natural substrates like plants, algae, or microorganisms [39]. Furthermore, plant by-products and waste have recently emerged as potent candidates for the synthesis of CDs, offering carbon-rich sources and cheap, easily accessible substrates. This approach supports the circular economy and sustainability by decreasing material consumption, reusing waste, and saving resources. Thus, in this research, we developed a new method for the synthesis of CDs utilizing rice straw as a source of carbon and used it as a self-ratiometric nanosensor for CPS.

Rice straw is the dry stalks of the rice crop (Oryza sativa) that is produced as a major by-product during rice harvesting. Thus, it is a very abundant waste that contains approximately 32–37 % cellulose, 29–37 % hemicellulose, 5–15 % lignin, and a minor percentage of inorganic compounds [40]. The world's annual production of rice is estimated to be 700 Mt. In the same vein, Egypt is considered the main rice producer in the Middle East; thus, a large amount of rice straw is produced annually. Farmers usually use open burning for disposal of rice straw to clear the field after harvest. With limited choices, open burning has intensified radically over the past decade, causing a significant environmental impact. Thus, there is a persistent need for enhanced technologies for rice straw management to moderate the environmental impact [41]. In this study, we developed a simple method for the synthesis of CDs by treatment of rice straw with NaOH at room temperature to initiate and achieve carbonization and quantum dots synthesis. This method furnished fluorescent CDs with high stability, water solubility, and excellent fluorescence characters with a large Stokes shift (about 70 nm). These properties endow the rice straw-derived CDs (RS-CDs) as a fluorescence sensor for rapid determination of CPS in different environmental water samples. Interestingly, CPS quantitatively quenched the fluorescence of RS-CDs at 396 nm while a new fluorescence peak appeared at 340 nm, which is enhanced by increasing CPS concentrations. This unique behavior has been utilized to develop an accurate double-reverse-signal ratiometric fluorescence probe for trace detection of CPS in irrigation, river, and drinking water samples via simply synthesized metal-free CDs. The ratiometric fluorescence sensors present an integrated self-calibration signal correction for more accurate and consistent analyte detection. Ratiometric probes have been steered to significantly improve the sensitivity, selectivity, and accuracy of sensing [42–46]. Additionally, a greenness, whiteness, and blueness assessment of the developed method is the main segment of this study, which explores the impact of the method on the environment and humans.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental
Instrument
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence experiments were done at room temperature at 600 nm/min speed of scan and 800 V sensitivity utilizing a fluorescence spectrophotometer fitted with a Xenon lamp (Cary Eclipse, Agilent Technologies, Santa Clara, USA) using a 1 cm quartz cell and excitation and emission monochromators slit width of 5 nm. UV-VIS absorption spectra were recorded by a Shimadzu Spectrophotometer (Kyoto, Japan). A thermostatic water bath (Cambridge Ltd, UK), a centrifuge (2-16P, Germany), and an ultrasonic bath (SS 101 H 230, Eumax, USA) were used. The buffer pH was measured by a pH meter, model NV P‐901, from Consort (Belgium). Transmission electron microscope (JEM-2100 TEM, JEOL, Japan), FTIR spectrometer (Nicolet - iS20, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA), emission electron microscope from a JEOL (JSM-6510LV) (Japan), and K-Alpha X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) were used for characterization of the RS-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemicals and solutions
ce:section.ce:para: The carbon precursor, rice straw, was bought from a farm in Meet Khames district (Dakahlia, Egypt). CPS (48 %) purchased from Alshark for Agrochemicals Co. LTD (Egypt). Absolute Methanol (MeOH) HPLC grade, hydrochloric acid (HCl), acetic acid (100 %), phosphoric acid (85 %), boric acid (99 %), and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were purchased from Merck (Germany).
Britton–Robinson buffer (BRB) was prepared by adjusting the pH of a solution containing 0.04 M boric acid, 0.04 M phosphoric acid, and 0.04 M acetic acid with 0.2 M NaOH to pH 2.0–12.0.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of RS-CDs from rice straw
ce:section.ce:para: Rice straw was repeatedly washed with distilled water and dried. After drying, the obtained raw material was crushed into a fine powder using a laboratory grinder. An amount of 5.0 g of the powder was weighed and blended with NaOH (100 mL, 1.25 M), followed by continuous stirring to allow complete impregnation of the powder with the alkali solution. The mixture was allowed to stand at room temperature for 2 h. The brown supernatant was aspirated, filtered with Whatman filter paper, and neutralized by HCl (1.25 M) to pH 7.0. The solution obtained was centrifuged (6000 rpm, 30 min), and the supernatant was collected and kept in the refrigerator (4 °C) in an amber-colored bottle.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the RS-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: TEM images were recorded to explore the shape and particle size of the RS-CDs using a TEM microscope operating at 200 kV. FTIR spectrum of the RS-CDs was scanned to navigate the surface functional groups. Elemental composition was also investigated with EDX software linked to an emission electron microscope operating at 200 kV. Characterization of the surface chemical composition of the RS-CDs was done by K-Alpha X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) utilizing monochromatic Al Kα radiation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calculation of quantum yield of the RS-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The fluorescence quantum yield of the RS-CDs Ф was examined using quinine sulfate (Ф = 54 % in 0.1 M H2SO4) as reference standard. The synthesized N,S-CQDs Ф was measured with the aid of the following formula [47]:

where F is the integrated fluorescence, A is the absorbance, ƞ is the refractive index, x denotes CQDs, and s is the standard quinine sulfate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure for determination of CPS by the RS-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Into a series of 10 mL volumetric flasks, varying amounts of CPS (5 ×10−3 M), 0.75 mL of pH 6.0 BRB, and 0.1 mL of RS-CDs were added. The solutions were completed with methanol to the mark and mixed well to obtain final concentrations of CPS (7.0 × 10−6–3.5 × 10−4 M). The fluorescence (F) was measured at 340 and 396 nm following excitation at 325 nm. Each experiment was run in three replicates (n = 3). The average ratios of fluorescence intensity at 340 and 396 nm, respectively (F340/396), are plotted versus the concentration of CPS [µM], and the regression equation was computed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Screening CPS in different environmental waters
ce:section.ce:para: Three water samples from different sources, including a tap (from our laboratory), irrigation water (Shrinquash village, Dakahlia, Egypt), and Nile River (Mansoura, Egypt), were obtained to assess the applicability of RS-QDs fluorescence sensor to determine CPS in real water samples. The water effluents were filtrated by a 0.22 µm syringe filter to remove any particulate matter. Next, 3.0 mL of each water sample was transferred to a set of 10 mL volumetric flasks and mixed with 0.1 mL of RS-CDs and 0.75 mL of pH 6.0 BRB. Methanol was added to make up the final volume to 10 mL. The ratios of fluorescence intensity at 340 and 396 nm, respectively (F340/396), were measured to screen for the existence of CPS in the water samples. The control experiment was conducted using distilled water instead of water samples. All measurements were in triplicates (n = 3).

For CPS spiking experiments, 3.0 mL of each water sample was spiked with CPS (10.0, 20.0, and 80.0 µM). Next, 0.1 mL of RS-CDs and 0.75 mL of pH 6.0 BRB were added. Methanol was added to make up the final volume to 10 mL. The average ratios of fluorescence intensity at 340 and 396 nm, respectively (F340/396), were measured to assess the matrix effect, and CPS recovery from different water samples was calculated. The measurements were in triplicates (n = 3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results and discussion
Synthesis of RS-CDs via room temperature alkali treatment
ce:section.ce:para: Rice straw has been utilized in this work as a raw material for the synthesis of RS-CDs. It is a left-over by-product of rice production at crop time. The richness of rice straw in carbon and oxygen that copiously exist in cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin components [40] made it a very appropriate choice for CDs’ synthesis. A simple and efficient synthesis procedure has been developed via alkali-assisted treatment at room temperature. The procedure does not require acidic catalysis, external heating, or extra energy input. The synthesis procedure has been optimized by varying the ratio of RS: NaOH (1:1, 1:2, 1:3, and 1:4, w/w). From Fig. 1a , RS: NaOH (1:3, w/w) gave a slightly higher quantum yield than ratio 1:1, w/w (Ф 4.35 versus Ф 4.9). Yet, we preferred to use 1:1 w/w (RS: NaOH) to avoid any harmful effects of using a higher NaOH concentration, especially since it slightly increases the Ф value.

Furthermore, the treatment time was evaluated from 1 to 48 h. The maximum fluorescence was achieved after 2 h of alkali treatment, and further time prolongation had no influence on the fluorescence, indicating that the synthesis was almost complete after 2 h. Initially, the synthesis mixture has a yellow color that turns brown with time. A summary of the synthesis procedure is illustrated in Fig. 1b.

According to the study of Krochta et al. [48], polysaccharides from agricultural products decompose to organic carboxylic acids under alkaline conditions. Considering that the main constituents of rice straw are polysaccharides such as cellulose, which is a polymer of glucose; hemicellulose, which is a copolymer consisting of glucose, galactose, mannose, and xylose, and lignin that is a heterogenous polymer derived from lignols precursor, it is believed that the alkali treatment of rice straw degrades the polysaccharides into smaller molecules such as carboxylic acids. Next, the organic carboxylic acids-NaOH mixture underwent nucleation and growth, where CDs are formed without any need for external heating or energy. It is worth noting that mixing NaOH with water produces a large amount of heat that may initiate the carbonization process [49]. This approach is corroborated by the results of Zheng et al. [49] and Li et al. [50] studies for room temperature synthesis of CDs by NaOH treatment of small molecules.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural characterization of the prepared RS-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology and size of the RS-CDs have been investigated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The TEM image of the RS-CDs (Fig. 2a ) showed evenly distributed and well-dispersed CDs with a sphere-shaped morphology. Besides, the obtained histogram (Fig. 2b) shows that the RS-CDs have a very minute size and narrow particle size distribution from 2.2 to 5.5 nm with an average particle size of 3.0 ± 0.7 nm.

Infra-red (IR) spectroscopy has also been used to elucidate the surface functional groups of the RS-CDs. The obtained IR spectrum (Fig. 2c) exhibits a distinct, strong, broad peak at 3319 cm−1 that refers to the OH stretching of alcohol or carboxylic groups [51]. The peak at 1631 cm−1 corresponds to the CC stretching of alkene/CO [51], while the weak peak at 2128 cm−1 is attributed to the CC stretching of alkyne [31].

The elemental composition of the RS-CDs has also been confirmed by Energy Dispersive X-ray analysis (EDX). The EDX (Fig. 2d) showed that the RS-CDs were composed mainly of carbon (63.39 %) and oxygen (20.48 %) in addition to sodium (9.19 %) and chloride (6.66 %).

The XPS results agreed well with these outcomes. The XPS scan showed that the RS-CDs were composed mainly of carbon and oxygen. This composition has recently been reported for CDs prepared from similar materials like wheat straw [51]. The high-resolution spectrum of C1s region showed three major peaks at 284.8, 286.2, and 288.1 eV corresponding to CC, CO, and COO− [51–54], respectively (Fig. 3a ). Meanwhile, the high-resolution spectrum of the O1s zone showed three chief peaks at 531.3 (CO), 532.8 (CO) [51], and 536.0 (Na KLL) eV (Fig. 3b) [55–57].

Thus, it can be concluded that the RS-CDs exhibit a very tiny size (3.0 ± 0.7 nm) and aromatic structure. RS-CDs are composed mainly of carbon and oxygen with surface carboxylic and hydroxyl functional groups that endow excellent polarity and water solubility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical characterization of the prepared RS-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The optical characteristics of RS-CDs have been studied using different techniques. The UV absorption spectrum (Fig. 3c) of the RS-CDs showed two bands at 200 and 282 nm that refer to π-π* transition of the sp2 conjugated carbon CC of the CDs’ aromatic core. The tiny shoulder at 312 nm is attributed to the n–π* transition of the hybridization with a heteroatom such as O of surface functional groups. This behavior was observed in recently reported literature for CDs prepared from similar precursors like wheat straw [58] and rice husk [59], which showed optical absorption in the ultraviolet region, extending a little in the visible range. Furthermore, photoluminescence is one of the most fascinating features of CDs. The π-π* transition on the aromatic ring accounts for the fundamental source of fluorescence emission of CDs. Besides, oxygen-containing functional groups on the carbon core are the main surface states of CDs that contribute to surface state fluorescence emission [60]. The RS-CDs exhibited an intense fluorescence at 396 nm upon excitation at 325 nm (Fig. 3c). The fluorescence quantum yield of RS-CDs was calculated using quinine sulfate as a reference standard, and it was found to be about 5 %. The obtained quantum yield is comparable to that of reported CDs prepared from similar materials, such as wheat straw [51].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle and mechanism of CPS ratiometric fluorescence sensing by the RS-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The mechanism of this ratiometric sensing toward CPS has been explored. First, we explored the mechanism that drives the fluorescence quenching at 396 nm. Static quenching, dynamic quenching, fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), and inner filter effect are reported fluorescence quenching mechanisms [25,44,61–63]. We collected the absorption spectrum of CPS and the excitation and emission spectra of RS-CDs (Fig. 4a ). Certain overlap has been detected between the absorption spectrum of CPS and the excitation spectrum of RS-CDs. This demonstrates that FRET does not affect the system since it comes into effect when the quencher absorption spectrum and fluorophore emission spectrum overlap [64]. However, the overlap between the absorption spectrum of CPS and the excitation spectrum of RS-CDs indicates that primary IFE has a role in fluorescence quenching [24,26,30,43,65]. To validate this conclusion, the fluorescence intensity of the RS-CDs was corrected for IFE after the addition of increasing concentrations of CPS. This was carried out using Eq. (1).

where: Fcorr and Fobs are the corrected and observed fluorescence. Aem and Aex are the absorbance values of the studied drugs at the emission and excitation wavelengths, respectively.

The suppressed efficiency (%E) of the corrected and observed fluorescence was also computed following Eq. (2):

where % E refers to the suppressed efficiency, F0 represents the fluorescence intensity of the blank, and F is the Fcorr or Fobs.

The plot of %E for both corrected and observed CDs fluorescence intensities against the concentrations (µM) of CPS (Fig. 4b) showed that subtraction of the IFE led to a loss of about 50 % of the suppressed efficiency (%E). This result confirmed that IFE has a role in the CDs' fluorescence quenching by CPS. However, this result also reveals the existence of another mechanism responsible for the remaining 50 % of the suppressed efficiency.

Thus, other mechanisms, including dynamic or static quenching, were also examined using the Stern-Volmer Eq. (3) as follows:
(3)

K
s v
[ Q ]
where (F0) and (F) represent the fluorescence intensities of RS-CDs in the absence and presence of the studied analyte, respectively, KSV is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant, and [Q] is the CPS molar concentration.

Besides, the suggested method was tested at three different temperatures (293, 303, and 313 K) to investigate the quenching mechanisms that may accompany IFE. In dynamic quenching, the KSV constant increases with temperature; in static quenching, on the other hand, it decreases with temperature, and in the case of IFE, it remains unaffected by temperature. At 293, 303, and 313 K, the KSV values were determined to be 12.33 × 103, 11.25 × 103, and 9.72 × 103 L.mol−1, respectively, suggesting a static quenching process (Fig. 4c).

The results of the quenching mechanism study confirmed the participation of both IFE and static quenching via complex formation. The formed complex leads to a decrease of the RS-CD fluorescence signal at 396 nm (turned-off signal) with the appearance of a new peak at 340 nm (turned-on signal).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of CPS detection conditions
ce:section.ce:para: The experimental conditions that may affect the sensing of CPS have been carefully studied. The effect of pH has been investigated over the range of 2.0–10.0 using BRB. The highest ratiometric response signal was obtained at pH 6.0 (Fig. 5a ). The impact of the buffer volume was investigated using different volumes of pH 6.0 BRB (0.2–2.0 mL). The best response was obtained using 0.75 mL BRB (Fig. 5b). In addition, the influence of RS-CDs volume on the ratiometric signal has been studied. The highest response was achieved using 0.1 mL RS-CDs (Fig. 5c).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Method validation
ce:section.ce:para: The validity of the developed method was evaluated using the International Conference on Harmonization Guidelines [66]. As discussed earlier, the addition of increasing concentrations of CPS to RS-CDs is associated with quenching of the fluorescence at 396 and enhancing the fluorescence at 340 nm (Fig. 4d). The linearity was assessed over the concentration range of 7.1 – 142.4 µM satisfying the regressing equation: F340/396 = 0.0095 [CPS] + 0.5116 with correlation coefficient of 0.9991, where F340/396 is the ratio of fluorescence intensity at 340 and 396 nm, respectively and [CPS] is the concentration of CPS [µM]. The corresponding calibration curve is illustrated in Fig. 4e.

The limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ) of the proposed method was determined according to the ICH guidelines [66] as 3 Sa /b and 10 Sa /b, respectively, where b is the slope of the calibration curve, and Sa is the standard deviation of the response. The LOD and LOQ were found to be 2.3 and 6.9 µM, respectively. Furthermore, the inter-day and intra-day accuracy and precision were evaluated over three consecutive days and three times per single day, respectively. The results obtained (Table 1 ) revealed high accuracy (98.83 to 102.62 %) and high precision (RSD, % ≤1.13) of the method.

To validate the accuracy and reliability of the proposed method, the results obtained have been statistically compared with those obtained by a reference method [7] by student t-test and Variance ratio F-test. The results illustrated in Table 2 confirmed that there are no significant differences between the two methods regarding accuracy and precision, respectively, since the calculated values of t- and F- are less than the tabulated values [67]. These results ascertained the reliability and accuracy of the developed method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anti-interference study
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the selectivity of the RS-CDs for CPS, different interfering species, including some insecticides like malathion, dimethoate, fipronil, and fenoxaprop-p-ethyl (Fen), anions, like Cl−, Br−, SO4 2− and acetate, and cations, like Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Pb2+, Ba2+, Co2+, Zn2+, Ni2+, and Cd2+ have been added to RS-CDs at final concentration of 1000 µM. It was found that the ratiometric fluorescence signal F340/396 did not significantly change in the presence of these potential interferents where the %change did not exceed 2 %. Fig. 6 demonstrates the results obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Screening of CPS in different environmental waters: insilico toxicity study and nanosensing
ce:section.ce:para: CPS is a wide-field typical organophosphorus insecticide. It is globally used to control flies, mosquitoes, several crop pests, home pests, and aquatic larvae. The extensive use of CPS has resulted in water contamination and a high detection rate in surface water [68]. CPS showed developmental toxicity and neurotoxicity, in addition to organophosphate-induced delayed polyneuropathy that is recounted some weeks after the poisoning occurrence [69]. Exploring the ADMET properties of CPS by the AdmetSAR tool [70] showed several aspects (Table 3 ). CPS can permeate the blood-brain barrier, indicating its potential CNS effects. The AdmetSAR metabolism predictor that is based on five CYP450 isoforms (CYP450 2C9, CYP450 2D6, CYP450 3A4, CYP450 1A2, and CYP450 2C19) has been used. The AdmetSAR suggests that CPS is a substrate of CYP450 2D6 and CYP450 3A4. While CPS has been revealed to be an inhibitor of CYP450 1A2, CYP450 2C19, and CYP450 3A4, showing high CYP450 inhibitory promiscuity with a 0.5334 probability. This effect is probably a main source of drug-drug interactions. Furthermore, the AdmetSAR manifests the high toxicity of CPS, as revealed by the fish, Tetrahymena Pyriformis, and Honeybee toxicity models. Additionally, CPS exhibited acute oral toxicity (category II compound). The non-biodegradability of CPS is another main dangerous factor that is revealed by the AdmetSAR tool that leads to accumulation in the environment. This insilico toxicity study exposed the dangerous influences that probably take place on exposure of human, organisms, or environment to CPS.

These dominant toxicity aspects amend the importance of the developed accurate, rapid, and sensitive ratiometric fluorescence probe for monitoring the presence of CPS in different environmental water samples. The screening study results showed that the water samples examined did not contain CPS. Thus, we carried out spiking experiments to reveal the influence of water matrix components on the assay of CPS (Table 4 ). The excellent recovery % in different water samples showed the reliability, accuracy, and efficiency of the developed probe for determining CPS residues in different water samples.

The validity of the method was assessed by evaluating its accuracy, intra-day precision, and inter-day precision in the three studied water samples to ensure its performance. The results presented in Table 5 indicate the excellent recovery and small values of RSD, which confirm the accuracy and precision of the method developed in different water samples. The results of the validation study confirmed the suitability of the method for application in CPS monitoring and analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The new ratiometric fluorescence probe versus reported literature for CPS determination by carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: Exploring the features of the published literature regarding the CDs-based determination of CPS showed the significant merits of our ratiometric fluorescence probe. As presented in Table 6 , the reported methods need a lengthy synthesis process and very high temperatures [14,17–20]. Besides, some of the reported methods utilized chemical substrates to synthesize CDs, such as trisodium citrate, dipotassium hydrogen phosphate and urea [18], gallic acid [19], or gelatin [20]. Furthermore, the reported methods usually depend on special instrumentations for the synthesis process, like an autoclave, while the new method does not need a specific instrument since it depends on maceration with NaOH. On the other hand, the developed probe is synthesized from a green and cost-efficient substrate using an energy-less procedure. Despite the reported methods showing better sensitivity for CPS than the developed method, the developed method outweighs them in terms of rapidness, simplicity, and easy steps. Besides, the developed method showed better accuracy expressed as the recovery %, and better precision indicated by RSD% as revealed from the data presented in Table 6.

Boosting the value of plant waste via upcycling rice straw to luminescent CDs is a significantly important action that helps sustain the practice in limited-income labs. Another prominent merit of the suggested synthesis approach is the energy-less nature that supports the sustainability and practicality of the developed method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Consideration of the eco-impact of the proposed method: AGREE, ComplexGAPI, and BAGI tools
ce:section.ce:para: Three recent tools have been utilized to assess the greenness of the developed method. AGREE is an Analytical GREEnness calculator that comprehensively evaluates the 12 principles of green analytical chemistry, giving an easily interpretable and informative pictogram with a score and color expressing the greenness of the method [71]. The obtained score for the developed method is 0.65, with a green central color (Fig. 7a ) showing the green nature of the developed method. Additionally, ComplexGAPI (Complementary green analytical procedure index), a tool that allows judging as much information as possible about the analytical method through counting pre-analysis steps, was used to assess method greenness [72]. The obtained pictogram (Fig. 7b) signifies the greenness of the developed method, as revealed by the obtained green fields.

In addition, the blueness of the method has also been investigated utilizing BAGI software (Blue applicability grade index). BAGI is a new tool for assessing the practicality of analytical methods that can be regarded as a complementary greenness assessment tool [73]. The obtained BAGI asteroid pictogram with a final score of 80 and multiple dark blue fields (Fig. 7c) proves the high compliance of the developed method with the assessed criteria, indicating the applicability of the method in terms of practicality.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusions
ce:section.ce:para: Luminescent CDs have been synthesized from the upcycling of rice straw as abundant and green plant waste via room temperature energy-free alkali treatment pathway. A characterization study revealed the elemental composition of the RS-CDs (carbon, oxygen, chlorine, and sodium). The synthesized RS-CDs proved to be a unique ratiometric fluorescence probe for the widely used insecticide CPS based on double-reverse-signal since CPS causes the fluorescence peak of RS-CDs at 396 nm to decline along with the appearance of a new peak at 340 nm. The ratiometric fluorescence probe showed excellent efficiency in the residual determination of CPS in tap, river, and irrigation water with %recoveries of 102.24 ± 1.52, 104.33 ± 1.79, and 100.43 ± 2.51, respectively. The F340/396 is linearly correlated to CPS concentration from 7.1 to 142.4 µM with a detection limit of 2.3 µM. The new method showed significant merits over the reported literature for CPS concerning greenness, feasibility, cost-efficiency, and practicality. Furthermore, the result of this work has a role as real action for environmental preservation and defeating the problem of rice straw burning, which is a significant threat to the environment and health.
